content.text: TypeScript ships three utility types that transform the modifiers on all properties of a type:
removes optional from every property, and
modifier to every property. All three are
— they preserve existing modifiers beyond the one they change.

attrs.alt: Partial makes all props optional, Required removes optional, Readonly adds readonly to all props

. Use it when you need a subset of fields — the most common case is a PATCH payload or a configuration defaults object.
. Use it when you have a type with optional fields but need a fully-populated version — for example, after merging defaults with user config.
constraint — the JavaScript runtime has no concept of readonly, so you can still mutate with
or at runtime. Use it for immutable view of data passed down through services or resolvers.
The built-in utilities only operate one level deep. For deeply nested structures, write recursive variants:
— makes every property optional. Use for PATCH payloads and configuration overrides.
— removes optional from every property. Use for resolved defaults and fully-populated objects.
— adds readonly to every property. Use for immutable snapshots and read-only function parameters.

content: All three are homomorphic — they iterate over keyof T and preserve other modifiers (nesting, readonly, optional) beyond the one they change.
They only operate one level deep — write recursive variants (DeepPartial, DeepRequired, DeepReadonly) for nested structures.
In Angular: Partial for HTTP PATCH, Required for form defaults, Readonly for component inputs and immutable state views.
